caveats: a: Broad NAICS 333 occupation proxy.
a: Product and channel mix vary substantially.
rho: No observed industry implementation rate.
rho: Safety-critical and regulatory outputs require review.
e: Injected firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
e: Consumer-brand sales do not always fit the recurring outsourced-service lens.
s5: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
s5: Very small denominator creates high statistical volatility.

q: Branded battery platforms retain more than retailer-controlled private label.

o: This does not measure employment retained within manufacturing.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Lawn and Garden Tractor and Home Lawn and Garden Equipment Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat equipment, attachment, and parts sales through retail, dealer, private-label, OEM, and direct channels, supported by configuration, warranty, parts, compliance, and customer service.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, retailers without manufacturing, repair-only businesses, financing entities, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers of powered lawnmowers, lawn and garden tractors, tillers, shredders, yard vacuums, leaf blowers, related attachments, and replacement products sold repeatedly to external retailers, dealers, brands, landscapers, or consumers.

a.bridge: BLS machinery-manufacturing data identify engine and machine assemblers and broader production roles, but do not isolate 333112. I translate this occupation mix into tasks: product-content creation, configuration, quoting, demand planning, procurement, engineering search, warranty triage, retailer support, and quality-data analysis are exposed; machining, molding, welding, assembly, painting, inspection, testing, maintenance, and material handling are less exposed. The broad proxy may understate consumer-channel office work or overstate exposure in assembly-heavy plants.
caveats: Broad NAICS 333 occupation proxy.
Product and channel mix vary substantially.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from the limited LMM population, split by production, engineering, marketing, retail support, warranty, and supply chain, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: Consumer and dealer information flows create moderate AI opportunity, while physical manufacturing remains the majority constraint.

caveats: Official sources establish product scope and technology transition but not a five-year quantity forecast.
Pandemic-era purchases may create a replacement-cycle distortion.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit forecasts by mower, tractor, blower, trimmer, and other equipment, split by battery and combustion, plus retailer sell-through and installed-base age, would move the estimate.
d5.rationale: Replacement of an installed base, household landscaping, and conversion from combustion to battery equipment support unit demand, while housing cycles, durable-product saturation, quieter landscaping practices, imports, and longer battery-tool lives constrain growth.

caveats: Injected firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
Consumer-brand sales do not always fit the recurring outsourced-service lens.
e.change_evidence: Screen all 24 injected candidates for ownership, manufacturing status, private-label/OEM revenue, repeat parts and dealer sales, EBITDA, product platform, and channel concentration.
e.rationale: The supplied population is small and likely contains strategic subsidiaries, captive plants, brand-dominant consumer businesses, and firms without a recurring outsourced-service relationship. Independent private-label, OEM, parts, and repeat dealer suppliers fit better, while concentrated or obsolete combustion-only operators may not.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333112 ($942,361,528) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333112 ($8,972,551,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $491,324 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333112); class <5: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $933,109 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $183,076 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,656,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $324,963 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,795,176 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $744,614 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,342,833 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,833,064 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $63,913,214 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,539,773 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $701,028,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $137,541,818 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 24

caveats: Operator share may migrate to overseas or vertically integrated battery-tool suppliers.
This does not measure employment retained within manufacturing.
o.change_evidence: Household and landscaper surveys on lawn area, service outsourcing, equipment ownership, sharing, imports, and battery adoption would move the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Consumers and landscapers still need physical powered equipment, and the shift to electric changes the product rather than replacing manufacture. Some demand can be eliminated through reduced lawns, outsourced landscaping, manual tools, imports, or equipment sharing.

caveats: No public contract sample measures benefit sharing.
Branded battery platforms retain more than retailer-controlled private label.
q.change_evidence: Retailer and dealer agreements, private-label bids, promotion and rebate histories, warranty economics, channel margins, and price-cost bridges would directly test retention.
q.rationale: Owned brands, battery ecosystems, parts compatibility, and differentiated products can preserve savings. Large retailers, dealer margins, private-label tenders, seasonal promotions, transparent consumer pricing, and intense product competition drive pass-through.

caveats: No observed industry implementation rate.
Safety-critical and regulatory outputs require review.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots across retailer content, support, warranty, parts forecasting, compliance documentation, and production planning at representative firms would move the range.
rho.rationale: Catalog content, customer support, warranty triage, parts identification, forecasting, quoting, and engineering-document tools are deployable within five years. Seasonal demand, retailer integrations, legacy systems, product safety, battery transition, and validation constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age chartbooks cover small firms generally, not 333112 transfers. I use age as succession pressure and discount more heavily for a tiny population, strategic ownership, product-platform dependence, retailer concentration, internal succession, and closures. Electrification-driven consolidation could raise acquisitions, while transition risk may cause wind-downs instead.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
Very small denominator creates high statistical volatility.
s5.change_evidence: Ownership histories and control deals from PitchBook, CapIQ, Grata, retailer supplier lists, and sector advisers since 2015 would establish a denominator-based rate.
s5.rationale: Succession can create deals, but few eligible firms, dominant strategic manufacturers, retailer dependence, product liability, and technology transition restrict qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Battery-platform transition and repeat dealer, warranty, parts, and customer workflows create a practical knowledge-work opportunity.
principal_weakness: A tiny eligible firm pool and powerful retail channels constrain acquisition supply and commercial retention.
